act_tone: Urgent, focused, Tony is all-business with moments of humor to keep morale up
jarvis_state: Minimal — short status reports, occasional glitches

mission.narrative: One light isn't enough. Build a distributed lighting array across the outpost.

sections.text: Wakey wakey Explorer the dawn of day three is upon us and as much as I'd love to let you Slumber in peace we have an Ocean's depth of work to tackle do you recall our incessantly blinking LED from yesterday well today we're going to show those painstaking glimmers of light who's boss enter the dip switch this Nifty little piece of Hardware will allow us to control the blinking at our will it's going to be the Relentless leash of our overzealous LED of course to make this happen we're going to need more than just some physical wiring this is where things get cerebral we're going to be introducing some conditional logic into our code a touch of if this then that all right then a flashy switch and some smart code are on our Horizon rest assured by the end of the day we'll have our blinking light under control so whenever you're ready explorer though regardless of your Readiness we must begin right let's not dilly dally our main character today is the dip switch this fellow might look simple with its array of tiny switches but oh can it wield a great deal of power you see dip stands for dual inline package it's essentially a ual electric switch that's packaged with others in a group the switches are all housed in a single box and can be toggled on or off independently you may think of this like a tiny panel of light switches each controlling a separate room in a miniature dollhouse each position of the individual switches in a dip switch represents a binary condition which simply means either on or off high or low one or zero yes or no the magic lies in the fact that you can manipulate these switches to control different parts of a circuit in our case the part of the circuit we're so Keen to dominate is that perpetually blinking LED by the end of today that led will be blinking only when we want it to the power of control is within our grasp Explorer so let's grab it allow me to introduce some vital elements the ground or gnd D and voltage or VCC they are you could say the lifeblood and nervous system of our Electronics ground my friend is the common point of reference for all voltage sources in a circuit it's our zero voltage reference line the foundation on which all other voltages are built without it our circuits would be aimlessly wandering in the wild uncertain about where the bottom of the voltage range lies it also provides a path for our electric currents to return to their Source after their exciting journey through our circuits now let's talk about VCC this little gem is the primary source of power to our heroboard it stands for voltage at the common collector in plain language it's the positive Supply voltage if gnd D is the foundation then VCC is the engine that powers the machine the fuel that keeps the circuits alive and kicking you see in a system like ours having a common ground such as gnd and a common power supply such as VCC is essential it allows different components of the system to communicate effectively each understanding what the zero and full points of voltage are this way when signals or power move between different parts of the system they all understand the language of voltage in the same way it's like our own language really if we didn't have common reference points and shared meanings we'd be unable to communicate and collaborate and we know that wouldn't end well would it Explorer moving on the the extra resistor a keen Observer aren't you Explorer you see the extra resistor we're talking about is what we call a pull down resistor its job is to make sure our circuit behaves properly when the switch is off without it our switch being in the off position could cause our circuit to get all wonky imagine it like a crowd of electricity not knowing where to go and causing a ruckus we certainly don't need more chaos around here what the pull down resistor does is finish the circuit and acts like a form of crowd control when the switch is off it guides the confused electricity safely away down to the ground this way our circuit stays composed and organized just the way we like it remember Explorer every brick in a building plays a vital role in keeping it standing similarly every small component in a system is crucial to its overall functionality and success now let's return to our task at hand now that we've solved the mystery of the pul down resistor let's turn our attention to something absolutely fundamental in electronics the concept of inputs and outputs think of our system as a living organism it needs to inhale data inputs to process and exhale data outputs as a result an input is essentially a message received by our HERO board it could be from a sensor a button or in our case today a dip switch these inputs tell the board what's happening out in the world in this instance whether our dip switch is on or off or high or low on the other hand outputs are signals sent out by the HERO board outputs could be triggered by code conditions or other sensor readings like the position of a switch examples include spinning a motor sounding a buzzer or making an LED blink or stay solid today our dip switch is the input telling the board whether the switch is on or high or off or low the LED is the output responding to this input by either blinking or staying off ah great we're done playing with our hardware for now Explorer don't look so relieved the fun is far from over you already know what's next programming our newly created circuit so grab another gulp of that precious oxygen and brace yourself for a thrilling journey into the intricacies of code let's power through explore the sooner we do this the sooner you can get some lights on in here and stop groping around in the dark well Explorer you've stumbled upon a fairly straightforward piece of code here let's dissect it together shall we first we're defining two variables one for our LED and one for our dip switch these numbers are currently configured to represent the PIN numbers on the HERO board where your switch and led are connected in the setup function we're telling the HERO board how to treat the two pins we defined earlier the pinMode parenthesis function is used for this we will make the LED an output meaning we want to send out a signal from this pin to control the LED we will make the dip switch an input meaning we expect to receive a signal from the switch to this pin next we move to the loop function the heart of our hero code that as you know runs continuously and repeatedly inside loop we have a new kind of statement an if statement I can see from the look on your face that Cadet training never taught you about conditional logic humans are such ineffectual creatures all right time for a bit of theory fear not explore it won't bite conditional logic involves creating rules within your code based on certain conditions much like deciding to wear a space suit before you step out into the vacuum of space simple would you agree the main tools for this in C+ plus language are if else if and else statements let's break them down if this is the primary decision making tool in your code if a certain condition is true then some code will run for example if oxygen levels are less than critical we initiate emergency procedures this only happens if the condition is true if the oxygen levels are measured to be fine the code within the if statement is ignored else if this comes into play when you have more than one condition to check it's like saying if the first condition isn't true and doesn't apply we'll evaluate the next one it's important to note that an else if always follows an if or another else if else this is the last resort if none of the previous if or else if conditions are true then the code in the else block will run it's like saying if nothing else works out let's do this here's a quick example you'll notice that we always indent everything inside sets of curly brackets this simply allows for better readability of the code in this code the ship checks the oxygen level and responds appropriately based on the conditions we've set I hope this brings some light into the murky depths of conditional Logic for you let's see if we can apply this to our switch situation next when the switch is on digitalWrite is executed turning the LED on or high as you could say this sends current to the LED if the switch is off the else block is executed instead here digitalWrite is run which grounds the LED's circuit turning it off so this code is continuously checking the state of your switch and turning the LED on or off accordingly quite easy don't you think now let's try this out on your end Explorer oh my I'll give you credit you have a Keen Eye good on you for noticing that you stumbled upon one of the classic conundrums of programming Explorer the taale of two equals they might look similar but they have different roles in the programming Universe let's clear up the confusion shall we in programming a single equal sign is known as the assignment operator it's used when you want to assign a value to a variable think of it as the word is in English for example int my variable equals 10 semicolon is like saying my variable is 10 now a double equal sign is a complet completely different Beast this is the equality operator and it's used to compare two values to see if they're equal if they are the comparison returns true if they're not it returns false this is used a lot in conditional statements like if statements here's an example so remember one equal sign is for assignment and double equal sign is for comparison it's crucial not to mix these up trust me it'll save you a lot of headache in the future ah it seems to be that time where we cut to the instructional material I was able to gather cast on the computer like the exceptional AI that I am I've also added this to your mission resources for future reference welcome back Explorer so far we've only used our heroboard to blink our onboard LED and as of yesterday we've also added the breadboard LED to Blink on and off however um this doesn't really show us all of the capabilities of what our hero can do in fact it only really scratches the surface not to mention a light that is always on is always going to be draining power even when we're asleep and that can't be good for your battery levels one of the largest strengths of a computer is the ability to examine inputs make decisions and then modify the outputs based on those decisions each of the pins on our hero can not only produce outputs they can also be used as inputs allowing the hero to read the current condition on a pin I want you to think of the HERO board as a mini computer which it technically actually is and realize how it compares to the computer that you're using right now right now you're using a keyboard and a mouse as inputs or potentially depending on what kind of device you're using potentially it might be a touchscreen or similar as an input device on your computer when you click something or you type something in and press enter that information is sent as an input into your computer your computer then does the exact same thing I mentioned before makes some calculations and then outputs the calculations on the screen using your graphics display drivers and you will visually be able to see them on your monitor your monitor is an output device your keyboard and your mouse are input devices and you can think of your computer is the equivalent of our HERO board however in the case of a heroboard we're going to be simplifying this instead of complex Mouse movements and large operating system calc calculations we'll be just be using a simple switch if a switch is on and you flip the switch on you can think of that as pressing a button on a keyboard the hero or the computer is going to then calculate that it needs to do something and then it's going to send as an output onto another pin in this case it's going to be the LED and then turn it on so tldr our sketch today will use another hero pin as an input to detect whether our switch is set to on or off once we read if it's supposed to be on or off we're going to turn the light on or off let's start by opening the sketch for day three as with all of our sketches the first multi-line comment just gives a lot of information about the sketch and if you are worried about it and it looks daunting poop you can close it just like that down below is a few additional Concepts that are going to be taught in this lesson we're going to be learning about the if statement the else control structure comparison operators and digital read as well as we're just going to talk a little bit about dip switches and resistors and if that's daunting to you too many words don't scared of it close it up you don't have to worry about it I should also mention that there is a few more comment blocks throughout here if you just want to go here close close close once you've collapsed all the comments if you so desire you want to what you can do now is you can include the uo. Which contains all the convenient definitions for our selected board in this case we have selected the Arduino Uno as that was compatible with our HERO board if you don't want to include this and you're using the Arduino IDE which is what I'm going to be using in this example you can technically hit delete and if you were to do something like that then you don't have to worry it will include it automatically so if you don't have it that's fine as long as you're using the uino IDE otherwise feel free to keep it it doesn't hurt anything anyway just as in our day two sketch we then need to define a constant to refer to the pin attached to our LED in this case we're going to be naming it cabin lights pin and we're going to connect it in our case to pin 12 on our HERO board then we're going to have another one that we're going to be defining and it's going to be the PIN for the switch the input device the cabin lights switch pin and we're going to connect that to the switch pin on pin two for our onetime setup this is where this is only run once we need to set up each of the pins to use as either an input or an output as you recall the lights the LED in this case is going to be an output and the switch is going to be an input so here's a switch that we're going to have that's going to be the input talk to the HERO board and then it's going to calculate and determine that we need to then turn off the LED on pin in this case 12 is an output after setup is run we will jump immediately to loop as the Arduino IDE and program language automatically does for us so we will jump into the loop function and in here we have a few different versions and this is where we have commented out because we wanted to show you iteratively how we got to this here this here is the commented out we won't be using it the first version of our Loop it works but it could be shorter more efficient and more understandable we commented it out just to show you how we can incrementally improve our code here we have the bite switch setting which is just simply going to save if it is on or off into a variable name if first we're going to say that the switch setting is equal to whatever we read off of the digital read on the cabin lights switch pin and this is where we can read the state of light and we're going to save it into a variable digital read is a function that quite literally does exactly as it says it reads the digital signal off of a specific pin and in this case we're going to be saving that whatever it returns into a variable here so if it is turned on it should be a one and if it is turned in the off position it will have a zero that is all that this function does it just simply determines is it reading A1 or is it reading a zero is there something being read on the pen or is there not and so when we save that to this here it will either have a one or a zero so let's just say if the switch setting is equal to high or in this case that's equal to one meaning that it's turned on if the switch if the switch is in the on position then we want to digitalWrite to the LED High basically turning it on what digitalWrite is doing here as we explained yesterday is it's simply sending a 5vt signal if it's high onto the pin or it's sending a ground signal if you set it to low which is exactly what we do here if on the other case it reads a signal on the pin and it the signal was low then we want to set the then we want to set it down to low that's the first way we can do it an improved version that is slightly better is we can remove an extra statement up here and we can actually add an if El statement so instead of using two if statements we can actually use an if else so we have bite switch setting equals digital read cabin lights switch pin this is the exact same as we were doing before just a little bit on condensed into one more line if the switch setting is equal to high then we can turn it on and then else you'll notice there's no else switch setting equal equal low because the only other condition it could be if it's not high in a digital setting is it's only can be low so we can actually remove that and simplify it ever so slightly here and even then with the if El statement Boop we can actually ignore that as well because there's actually technically a final version and this is the one where we're going to be using because it's probably the most simple way that we can Implement what was above we can literally have it as just one if El statement if digital read cabin lights switch pin is equal to high and in this case we're not using an additional variable because there's no reason to have an extra variable that's saved in memory if digital read cabin lights switch pin is equal to high then we're going to turn it on to high otherwise if it's not high then we're going to going to turn it to low and we're going to keep the light off and this is probably the most simple form of what we can get from the above so with this you should have all the code that is needed for day three with this new functionality you should be able to turn on and off the LED on your ship with only the time that you need it so that you're not wasting any battery in your critical condition that you are currently in best of luck and we'll see you again on day four welcome to the wiring example video for day three last time we saw how to use the Hero controller to raise the voltage on an output pin in order to turn on an LED today we want to build further on that idea with the aim of being able to turn the light on and off at will without needing to reload the software sketch the key to this is that in addition to using pin 12 12 as an output from the hero we will use pin two of the hero as an input according to the coding that we've provided in our sketch when pin two reads a high say or 5V potential or when it reads Low that is Zer volts the hero takes different actions on the output pin 12 there are two new points for us to understand here on the digital side the hero is repeatedly executing code in the loop routine and every time the loop is executed pin 12 is repeatedly tested and the status of pin 12 may or may not be changed the second point for us to to think about has to do with how we are going to provide a selectable high or low voltage state to pin 2 now in order to do this we've set up a small circuit on the left side here of our breadboard that runs from 5 Vols through an onoff switch which is this red component right here then through a high value resistor in this case the resistor has a value of 10,000 ohms or 10 kiloohms which will limit the current flowing through it to less than a milliamp and then finally back to ground to complete the circuit a quick note this switch is called a dip switch dip is an acronym for dual inline package and this is a new component for us this is actually three switches in one package uh but we'll only be using one switch number one the one on the left in today's project although in future projects will'll have occasion to use more than just one now reiterate ating our 5 volts is supplied to one side of the dip switch through this red wire that's connected to 5 volts on the hero now the switch in turn is connected to the high side of our 10 kilohm resistor and then that finally completes the uh circuit back to ground at the end now let's understand how this is actually going to work if the switch is in the on position then current will flow through the circuit and through our resistor the amount of current will be small but it will be an amount of current commensurate with a 5V potential difference between ground and this upper side of the resistor if we connect this point at the upper side of the resistor using this brown jumper wire if we connect it to pin two on the hero pin two will now read a high a 5vt high reading if current is flowing through the circuit on the other hand if we were to throw this switch into the off position breaking the circuit then no current would be flowing through and according to Ohm's law there would be no voltage difference across the resistor between its ground side and its upper side so both of those would be at zero volts and in that condition pin two connected by this brown wire to this point in the circuit would read a low so this is how by flipping the switch between on and off we can toggle the voltage at Pin two between being high and low now I I want to make a quick note here before we move on our selection here to use a brown wire was completely arbitrary depending upon what colors you have available in your parts kit you might choose to use a different color apart from trying to adhere to the rule of using red for positive Supply and black for ground you should feel free to use any combination of Jumper wire colors that best help you organize and understand your circuits there's Just One Last Detail to mention here before we go ahead and test this new circuit because we have more than one current Loop operating on our breadboard here we have need for more than one set of supply and ground connections and this is a situation that'll happen quite frequently and rather than providing separate connections for our two Loops here our two subcircuits will'll set up shared common lines or buses that can be tapped by any subcircuit on the breadboard that needs to you'll notice that the two rows along the top side and also along the bottom side of the breadboard are labeled with red and blue lines and they're special because they are connected horizontally all the way across rather than vertically like the columns in the main part of the breadboard and since they do connect horizontally all the way across they provide uh convenient access to any components that may need to access them from the main part of the breadboard notice that in this case we've really only used the blue bus right here on the top side of the breadboard and we've used that to distribute ground to our two subcircuits so what we have right now is a black wire connected from hero ground to one end of this ground bus and then we have two places where it's being used where it's being tapped off so we have a black wire connecting from this ground line to the lower end the low side of our LED so this is the ground side of the LED and we're also using the same ground line right here to connect to the bottom side the low voltage side of our logic resistor that's being used to provide a signal to go to pin 2 so finally we're ready to go ahead and test our day three circuit now now we're starting right now with switch one in the off position and so we'll upload our software sketch and it'll start to run but we would expect nothing to happen until we move the switch from open to close so let's go ahead and do that we'll go ahead go over to the console and upload the day3 script so it's loading all right the script is loaded now and it's running and we don't see any change of status until we go ahead and move the switch from open to close and so what we'd expect to happen when we do that is that pin two will go high the hero will detect that and turn pin 12 High to light the yell so let's go ahead and give that a try moving the switch to close and that works as expected and realize now that because the program is constantly polling pin 2's status over and over again we can turn it on and off as often as we like so I can turn the switch to off and the LED goes off and it will respond to any changes in the status of pin 2 so this uh all appears to be working correctly and this is just another small step but we're making significant progress next time we'll build even further on this and get even more complex control over our lighting circuits
